content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

September 6, 2000

Colleen C. O'Harra
City Councilmember, City of Oceanside
Greenman, Lacy, Klein, O'Harra & Heffron
900 Pier View Way
Oceanside, California 92049

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-174

Dear Councilmember O'Harra:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ You are seeking reconsideration of the Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-97-567 and the Bennett Advice Letter, No. A-99-307. You are basing your request on new facts, and you have clarified facts upon which these letters relied.² Please note that the analysis in this letter does not evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Reg. 18329(b)(8)(A).)

QUESTIONS

Your husband works for a real estate agency ("Lee & Associates") that does business in the city's redevelopment area. Your husband also has a client ("Somerset Development Company") that owns property in the redevelopment area.

1. Do you have a conflict of interest in a decision approving the construction of a theater in the redevelopment area (OliverMcMillan project) based on your economic interest in Lee & Associates?

2. Do you have a conflict of interest in the OliverMcMillan decision based on your economic interest in Somerset Development Company?

_______________

¹ See Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at California Code of Regulations, Title 2, Sections 18109-18996.

² This letter supersedes both the Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-97-567 and the Bennett Advice Letter, No. A-99-307 to the extent that they conflict with the advice in this letter. The legal analysis in both letters is still correct. However, you have presented a new set of facts that are significantly different from the facts that were presented on your behalf in the previous letters. This letter is based on the new facts that you have provided.

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 2

3. Do you have a conflict of interest in a decision approving a large-scale luxury resort
hotel in the redevelopment area (Manchester project) based on your economic interest
in Lee & Associates?

4. Do you have a conflict of interest in the Manchester decision based on your economic
interest in Somerset Development Company?

CONCLUSION

1. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase Lee &
Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more.

2. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase the fair
market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.

3. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
increase Lee & Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more.

4. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
increase the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.

FACTS

You are a member of the Oceanside City Council. The entire city council sits as the
Oceanside Community Development Commission. Two separate and independent projects are
currently pending before the development commission. The first project ("Manchester project")
involves the development of a luxury resort hotel with 500 rooms in twin 12-story towers,
including a separate conference/ballroom facility, and accessory retail shops. The gross area of
the project is approximately six city blocks.

The second project is the OliverMcMillan project, a 16-screen theater complex on two
blocks with accompanying restaurant and retail space. Both projects require the development
commission's approval of land use permits and development agreements.

Your spouse works for and has an investment interest of $1,000 or more in a real estate
firm ("Lee & Associates") that does business in the redevelopment area. Your spouse's interest
in the firm used to be 10 percent or more before April 1, 2000, but his interest is now less than
10 percent.

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 3

Your spouse is also a limited partner in Lee & Associates Venture Capital Partnership
NSDC. His investment interest in the partnership is worth $1,000 or more. The limited
partnership has more than 100 limited partners. It funded the opening of Lee & Associates in
Carlsbad. It does not have an ownership interest in Lee & Associates, but receives 6 percent of
the gross annual income of that business.

Clarified Facts

A previous request for advice stated that your spouse had a client who owned property in
the redevelopment area. The client was an individual whose name was Richard Cicoletti.
According to your new request for advice, the client is not an individual, but is a business entity,
the Somerset Development Company ("Somerset"). Somerset, rather than Mr. Cicoletti, owns
the property in the redevelopment area that is between 300 and 2,500 feet from the
OliverMcMillan project. Somerset paid your spouse its final commission in October 1999. Your
spouse does not have any other clients who own property in the redevelopment area. In addition,
no other clients who paid income to Lee & Associates before April 1, 2000, own property in the
redevelopment area.

New Facts

On July 28, 2000, you received a legal opinion from Leo Sullivan regarding whether you
had a conflict of interest in the redevelopment decisions based upon your spouse's business
interests. In that opinion, your attorney advised you that the redevelopment decisions would not
materially affect Lee & Associates. The letter stated as follows:

"Lee & Associates earned $16,517 in gross income from clients
whose properties were within redevelopment agency boundaries in
1999. For a decision Commissioner O'Harra participates in to be
material as to Lee & Associates, the company's commission
income from that area would have to increase by $10,000 in a one
year period, a sixty percent increase. Because Lee & Associates'
commissions are directly proportional to property values, assuming
a similar amount of work from listings in the jurisdiction in the
next twelve months, the property values in the redevelopment area
would also have to increase by about sixty percent in a one year
period in order to have this effect."

The legal opinion was ambiguous as to whether a decision regarding the OliverMcMillan
would affect the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000, but that even if it did, the
effect of the decision would not be distinguishable from the effect on other properties in the
redevelopment area. As to the Manchester project, the legal opinion concluded that because of

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 4

the distance between the Manchester project and the Somerset property, it is not reasonably
foreseeable that the decision would have a material financial effect on the Somerset property.

ANALYSIS

A. Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition

A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualifying
financial interest in a decision. (Reg. 18700(b).)

1. Are you a public official?

As a member of the Oceanside City Council and the Oceanside Community Development
Commission, you are a public official as defined by the Act. (Section 82048; Reg. 18701.)

2. Does the prohibition apply to your activities?

The prohibition applies when a public official makes, participates in making, or attempts
to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision. (Section 87100.) By
voting on a conditional use permit or development agreement, you will be "making a
governmental decision" and you will therefore be engaging in activity regulated by the Act.
(Reg. 18702.1.)

3. What are your economic interests?

A public official has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official,³ or on the following enumerated economic interests:

(1) Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth $1,000
    or more. (Section 87103(a).)
(2) Any real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
    more. (Section 87103(b).)
(3) Any source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months before
    the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
(4) Any business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
    or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

_________
³ For purposes of Section 87103, a decision will have a financial effect "on the official" if the decision
affects his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate family. (Reg.
18703.5.) We call this the "personal financial effect rule." The rule does not appear to apply to your facts.

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 5

(5) Any donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months before
the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

Under Section 87103(a), an official's investment interests include the investment
interests of his or her spouse. In addition, an official has a community property interest, or 50
percent interest, in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Therefore, you have an
economic interest in your spouse's investments and in 50 percent of his income.

The commission income that a real estate agent receives from real estate sales is
attributed to the following sources: (1) the broker and brokerage business entity under whose
auspices the agent works; (2) the person the agent represents in the transaction; and (3) anyone
who receives a finder's fee or other referral fee, or who makes a referral by contractual
arrangement. (Reg. 18703.3(c)(3)(C).)

Under your facts, you have two relevant economic interests. First, you have an indirect
investment interest in Lee & Associates worth $1,000 or more.⁴ (Section 87103(a).) In addition,
you have a community property interest in your spouse's commission income that is attributable
to Somerset and totals $500 or more during the last 12 months. (Section 87103(c).)

Once a public official identifies all relevant economic interests, the official must evaluate
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of those economic interests. This determination takes three steps, which we discuss as
Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine whether the economic interest will be
involved directly or indirectly in the decision. (Reg. 18700(b)(4).) Based upon the degree of
involvement, the official must then find the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Reg. 18700(b)(5).) After finding the applicable materiality standard,
the official must then decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
decision will meet the standard. (Reg. 18700(b)(6).)

4. Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?

A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision if the person
initiates, is a named party in, or is subject of, the decision. (Reg. 18704.1(a)(2).) A person is
the "subject of the decision" if the decision involves a license, permit, or contract with that
person. (Ibid.) If a person is not directly involved in the decision, then the person is indirectly
involved. (Reg. 18704.1(b).)

Neither Lee & Associates nor Somerset are named parties in the redevelopment
decisions. In addition, the decisions do not involve a license, permit or contract with either

⁴ You also have an indirect investment interest in Lee & Associates Venture Capital Partnership. The
limited partnership shares profits with Lee & Associates. Therefore, our conflict analysis for Lee & Associates will
also apply to the limited partnership.

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 6

company. Therefore, both Lee & Associates and Somerset are indirectly involved in the
redevelopment decisions for purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard.

5. What is the applicable materiality standard?

Commission Regulation 18705.1 sets forth the materiality standard for business entities
that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision, including those that are a source of
income to the public official.5 (Reg. 18705.3(b)(1).) These standards vary according to the size
of the business entity. The bigger the business, the greater the monetary impact must be for the
effect to be material.

For small business entities, such as Somerset and Lee & Associates, the applicable
materiality standard is Regulation 18705.1(b)(7).6 Under that regulation, the effect of a decision
is material if it will increase or decrease a company's: (1) gross revenues by $10,000 or more in
a fiscal year; (2) expenses by $2,500 in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities by
$10,000 or more.

6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?

A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially likely to occur.
(Reg. 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

a. Lee & Associates

With regard to Lee & Associates, you must decide whether it is substantially likely that
the decisions approving the Manchester and OliverMcMillan projects will increase or decrease
Lee & Associates': (1) gross revenues by $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; (2) expenses by
$2,500 in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more.

Under your facts, the relevant consideration is whether the redevelopment decisions will
increase Lee & Associates' gross revenue by $10,000 in a fiscal year. The anticipated result of a
redevelopment project is an increase in real estate values in the redevelopment area. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Downey Cares v. Downey Community Development Com.
(1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983.) Lee & Associates' commission income is based on a percentage of
the price at which property is sold. Thus, Lee & Associates' income will increase as the value of

5 The Commission is currently in the process of amending many of its conflict-of-interest regulations.
Regulation 18705.1 may become one of the amended regulations.

6 Regulation 18705.1(b)(7) business entities are companies that are not listed on the Pacific Stock
Exchange or the Eligible Securities List maintained by the Department of Corporations, and that do not meet the
qualifications to be listed on the NASDAQ Stock Exchange.

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 7

property in the redevelopment area increases. Last year, Lee & Associates' gross revenue that
was attributed to the redevelopment area was $16,517.

You believe that it is not substantially likely that Lee & Associates annual gross revenues
derived from the redevelopment area will jump to $26,517 or more (a $10,000 or more increase)
as a result of either the OliverMcMillan project or the Manchester project. If your estimation of
the probable financial impact of these decisions is a reasonable one, then your economic interest
in Lee & Associates will not cause a conflict of interest for you.

The Commission does not act as finder of fact when issuing advice.⁷ (In re Oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71, 77.) Therefore, we cannot advise you regarding whether your attorney's
assessment on which you rely is correct. We can advise, however, that an official may not
simply rely on a third-party appraisal without making further inquiry into whether the person
conducting the appraisal is qualified to do so, whether the appraisal considered all of the
appropriate factors, and whether the conclusion reached by the appraiser is objectively
defensible. (Kimbell Advice Letter, No. A-97-201.) Ultimately, as the public official with the
potential conflict, you bear the responsibility of deciding whether a material financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable. You may consult third parties when making that determination, and you
may rely on their opinion if it is reasonable to do so under the circumstances and your reliance is
in good faith. Here, there may be some question as to the objectivity of the appraiser. But if you
believe, in good faith, that your attorney's analysis and judgment are sound and that he
considered all of the factors that are relevant to your request, then you will not have a conflict of
interest in the redevelopment decisions based on your economic interest in Lee & Associates.

b. Somerset

As to Somerset Development Company, the question is the same—is it substantially
likely that either redevelopment decision will increase or decrease Somerset's: (1) annual gross
revenues by $10,000 or more; (2) annual expenses by $2,500; or (3) the fair market value of real
property or other assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more? Under these facts, the relevant
question is whether one of the decisions will increase the value of Somerset's real property by
$10,000 or more.

You believe that it is not reasonably foreseeable that a decision regarding the Manchester
project will increase the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 because Somerset's
property is not in close proximity to the Manchester project. If your estimation is reasonable,
then you will not have a conflict of interest in the Manchester project based on your economic
interest in Somerset. Again, you bear the ultimate responsibility of deciding whether the
applicable materiality standard will be met.

⁷ Formal written advice confers immunity (see Section 83114) only to the extent that the facts provided are
correct and the requestor has disclosed all of the material facts.

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 8

With regard to the OliverMcMillan project, your facts regarding the probable financial
effect is somewhat ambiguous.8 Therefore, we leave it to you to decide whether a decision on
the OliverMcMillan project will increase the value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.

7. Does the public generally exception apply?

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
the decision if the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "public generally" exception. For this
exception to apply, the decision must affect the official in substantially the same manner as it
would affect a significant segment of the public. (Reg. 18707.)

When the economic interest affected by a decision is a business entity, a "significant
segment" of the public is 50 percent of the businesses in the jurisdiction of the official's agency
or the district the official represents. (Reg. 18707(b)(1)(B).) In the legal opinion enclosed with your request, your attorney advised you that the public generally exception applies
to your facts. According to your attorney, at least 50 percent of the businesses in the
redevelopment area will be affected by the redevelopment decisions in substantially the same
manner as your economic interests. However, the jurisdiction of the city's community
redevelopment commission is the entire city, not just the redevelopment area.3 Therefore, to
determine whether the public generally exception applies to a redevelopment decision, you must
consider whether the decision will affect 50 percent of all of the businesses in the City of
Oceanside in substantially the same manner as the business entity that is causing the conflict.
You have not provided any facts regarding the public generally exception.

8. Does the Legally Required Participation Rule apply?

The eighth step is the "legally required participation" rule. (Reg. 18708.) This rule only
applies when several public officials in the same agency have a conflict of interest in the same
decision. The rule does not appear to be relevant to your facts.

_____________
8 The legal opinion written by your attorney concludes that a material financial effect is not reasonably
foreseeable. However, the associate memorandum that is attached to the legal opinion states:

"Because the company owns property in the redevelopment area, the likely way it could be affected by a
redevelopment decision is by a resulting increase in the value of its property of more than $10,000 resulting
from the decision. The appraised value of Somerset's property … is approximately $626,000, and $10,000
would represent only a 1.6% increase."

9 Under the Act, a local governmental agency's jurisdiction is the geographical area in which the agency
has jurisdiction. (Section 82035.) The jurisdiction of a city redevelopment agency is the city. (Health & Safety
Code § 33120.)

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 9

B. Interrelated and Implementing Decisions

When you are assessing the foreseeable impact of a decision, you must look at each
governmental decision independently. A finding of a conflict of interest at one stage of a project
does not necessarily mean that you will have a conflict of interest in every decision about that
project. However, a series of decisions may be too interrelated for you to consider separately,
and a conflict on one decision will be disqualifying for all of the other decisions. Two decisions
are inextricably interrelated when one decision is a necessary condition precedent or condition
subsequent for another.

Furthermore, we have advised that public officials may participate in decisions that
implement major policy decisions already made, despite their financial interests. However, they
may only participate in the implementing decisions if these decisions will not independently
have a material effect on their economic interests. (Curry Advice Letter, No. I-98-006.) For
example, in the Athan Advice Letter, No. A-86-094, we advised:

"[W]e conclude that Mayor Bennett must disqualify himself from
participating in decisions of the San Ramon City
Council/Redevelopment Agency concerning the proposed Crow
Canyon area. However, we emphasize that this conclusion applies
only to the major policy decisions about the project, such as project
boundaries, financing decisions, approval of the environmental
impact report, types of uses, and major public improvements in the
project area. Once the basic policy decisions have been reached,
Mayor Bennett may participate in the decisions which implement,
but do not change these policies."

Similarly, in the Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454, we advised that:

"If the decision is one which is critical to the project and could
result in its termination or significant modification, Mr. Abbott
must refrain from participating in it to the extent that the total
effect of the project will materially affect ...[an economic
interest]... according to the test set forth above. If the decision is
one which implements a decision previously made, the effect of
the decision could be measured by assessing the difference
between various implementation alternatives presented."

File No. A-00-174
Page No. 10

If you have any questions about a particular redevelopment decision or any other
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.conclusion:
1. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase Lee &
Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more.
2. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase the fair
market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.
3. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
increase Lee & Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more.
4. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
increase the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.

Your spouse is also a limited partner in Lee & Associates Venture Capital Partnership

foreseeable that the decision would have a material financial effect on the Somerset propeqty.
sections.analysis:
A. Conflict-of-Interest Prohibition
A public official may not make, participate in making, or in any way attempt to use his or
her official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official knows, or has
reason to know, that he or she has a financial interest. (Section 87100.) The Commission has
developed an eight-step approach for determining whether an individual has a disqualiffing
financial interest in a decision. (Reg. 18700(b).)
1. Are you a public official?
As a member of the Oceanside City Council and the Oceanside Community Development
Commission, you are a public official as defined by the Act. (Section 82048; Reg. 18701.)
2. Does the prohibition apply to your activities?
The prohibition applies when a public official makes, participates in making, or attempts
to use his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision. (Section 87100.) By
voting on a conditional use permit or development agreement, you will be "making a
governmental decision" and you will therefore be engaging in activity regulated by the Act.
(Reg. 18702.1.)
3. What are your economic interests?
A public official has a disqualifying financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official,3 or on the following enumerated economic interests:
(1) Any business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment worth S1,000
or more. (Section 87103(a).)
(2) AnV real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest worth $1,000 or
more. (Section 87103(b).)
(3) AnV source of income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months before
the decision. (Section 87103(c).)
(a) AnV business entity in which the official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
'ForpurposesofSection87103,adecisionwillhaveafinancialeffect"ontheofficial"ifthedecision
affects his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, or those of his or her immediate family. (Reg.
18703.5.) We call this the "personal financial effect ruIe." The rule does not appear to apply to your facts.


Under your facts, you have two relevant economic interests. First, you have an indirect
investment interest in Lee & Associates worth $1,000 or more.o (Section 87103(a).) In addition,
you have a community property interest in your spouse's commission income that is attributable
to Somerset and totals $500 or more during the last 12 months. (Section 87103(c).)
(5) AnV donor of gifts worth $300 or more provided to the official within 12 months before
the decision. (Section 87103(e).)
Under Section 87103(a), an official's investrnent interests include the investment
interests of his or her spouse. In addition, an official has a community property interest, or 50
percent interest, in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Therefore, you have an
economic interest in your spouse's investments and in 50 percent of his income.
The commission income that a real estate agent receives from real estate sales is
attributed to the following sources: (1) the broker and brokerage business entity under whose
auspices the agent works; (2) the person the agent represents in the transaction; and (3) anyone
who receives a finder's fee or other referral fee, or who makes a referral by contracfual
arangement. (Reg. I 8703.3(c)(3XC).)
Once a public official identifies all relevant economic interests, the official must evaluate
whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one
or more of those economic interests. This determination takes three steps, which we discuss as
Steps 4, 5 and 6. First, the official must determine whether the economic interest will be
involved directly or indirectly in the decision. (Reg. 18700(bX4).) Based upon the degree of
involvement, the official must then find the applicable materiality standard set forth in
Commission regulations. (Reg. 18700(bX5).) After finding the applicable materiality standard,
the official must then decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the
decision will meet the standard. (Reg. 18700(bX6).)
4. Is the economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the decision?
A person, including a business entity, is directly involved in a decision if the person
initiates, is a named party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Reg. 1870a.1(aX2).) A person is
the "subject of the decision" if the decision involves a license, permit, or contract with that
person. (Ibid.) If a person is not directly involved in the decision, then the person is indirectly
involved. (Reg. 18704.1(b).)
Neither Lee & Associates nor Somerset are named parties in the redevelopment
decisions. In addition, the decisions do not involve a license, permit or contract with either
o You also have an indirect investnent interest in Lee & Associates Venture Capital Parhrership. The
limited parhrership shares profits with Lee & Associates. Therefore, our conflict analysis for Lee & Associates will
also apply to the limited partnership.
company. Therefore, both Lee & Associates and Somerset are indirectly involved in the
redevelopment decisions for purposes of finding the applicable materiality standard.
5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
Commission Regulation 18705.1 sets forth the materiality standard for business entities
that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision, including those that are a source of
income to the public official.5 (Reg. 18705.3O)(l).) These standards vary according to the size
of the business entity. The bigger the business, the greater the monetary impact must be for the
effect to be material.
For small business entities, such as Somerset and Lee & Associates, the applicable
materiality standard is Regulation 18705.1(bX7).6 Under that regulation, the effect of a decision
is material if it will increase or decrease a company's: (1) gross revenues by $10,000 or more in
a fiscal year; (2) expenses by $2,500 in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities by
$10,000 or more.
6. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
A material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable if it is substantially likely to occur.
(Reg. 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
il. Lee & Associates
With regard to Lee & Associates, you must decide whether it is substantially likely that
the decisions approving the Manchester and OliverMcMillan projects will increase or decrease
Lee & Associates': (1) gross revenues by $10,000 or more in a fiscal year; (2) expenses by
$2,500 in a fiscal year; or (3) value of assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more.
Under your facts, the relevant consideration is whether the redevelopment decisions will
increase Lee & Associates' gross revenue by $10,000 in a fiscal year. The anticipated result of a
redevelopment project is an increase in real estate values in the redevelopment area. (In re
Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; Downey Cares v. Downey Communtty Development Com.
(1987) 196 Cal.App.3d 983.) Lee &. Associates' commission income is based on a percentage of
the price at which properfy is sold. Thus, Lee & Associates' income will increase as the value of
5 The Commission is currently in the process of amending many of its conflict-of-interest regulations.
Regulation 18705.1 may become one of the amended regulations.
6 Regulation 18705.1(bX7) business entities are companies that are not listed on the Pacific Stock
Exchange or the Eligible Securities List maintained by the Deparfrnent of Corporations, and that do not meet the
qualifications to be listed on the NASDAQ Stock Exchange.
property in the redevelopment area increases. Last year, Lee & Associates' gross revenue that
was attributed to the redevelopment area was 516,517.
You believe that it is not substantially likely that Lee & Associates annual gross revenues
derived from the redevelopment area will jump to $26,517 or more (a $10,000 or more increase)
as a result of either the OliverMcMillan project or the Manchester project. If your estimation of
the probable financial impact of these decisions is a reasonable one, then your economic interest
in Lee & Associates will not cause a conflict of interest for you.
The Commission does not act as finder of fact when issuing advice.T 1In re Oglesby
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 71, 77.) T\erefore;-we cannot advise you regarding whether your attorney's
assessment on which you rely is correct. We can advise, however, that an official may not
simply rely on a third-parfy appraisal without making further inquiry into whether the person
conducting the appraisal is qualified to do so, whether the appraisal considered all of the
appropriate factors, and whether the conclusion reached by the appraiser is objectively
defensible. (Kimbrell Advice Letter, No. A-97-201.) Ultimately, as the public official with the
potential conflict, you bear the responsibility of deciding whether a material financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable. You may consult third parties when making that determination, and you
may rely on their opinion if it is reasonable to do so under the circumstances and your reliance is
in good faith. Here, there may be some question as to the objectivity of the appraiser. But if you
believe, in good faith, that your attorney's analysis and judgment are sound and that he
considered all of the factors that are relevant to your request, then you will not have a conflict of
interest in the redevelopment decisions based on your economic interest in Lee & Associates.
b. Somerset
As to Somerset Development Company, the question is the same-is it substantially
likely that either redevelopment decision will increase or decrease Somerset's: (1) annual gross
revenues by $10,000 or more; (2) annual expenses by $2,500; or (3) the fair market value of real
property or other assets or liabilities by $10,000 or more? Under these facts, the relevant
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, ocr; CONCLUSION appears before QUESTION

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: is whether one of the decisions will increase the value of Somerset's real property by
$10,000 or more.
You believe that it is not reasonably foreseeable that a decision regarding the Manchester
project will increase the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 because Somerset's
property is not in close proximity to the Manchester project. If your estimation is reasonable,
then you will not have a conflict of interest in the Manchester project based on your economic
interest in Somerset. Again, you bear the ultimate responsibility of deciding whether the
applicable materiality standard will be met.
7 Formal written advice confers immunity (see Section 831 14) only to the extent that the facts provided are
correct and the requestor has disclosed all ofthe material facts.
With regard to the OliverMcMillan project, your facts regarding the probable financial
effect is somewhat ambiguous.8 Therefore, we leave it to you to decide whether a decision on
the OliverMcMillan project will increase the value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more
7. Does the public generally exception apply?
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still participate in
the decision if the effect of the decision is not distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally. (Section 87103.) This is known as the "public generally" exception. For this
exception to apply, the decision must affect the official in substantially the same manner as it
would affect a significant segment of the public. @eg. 18707.)
When thetsconomic interest affected by a decision is a business entity, a "significant
segment" of the public is 50 percent of the businesses in the jurisdiction of the official's agency
or the district the official represents. (Reg. 18707(bXlXB).) In the legal opinion that you
enclosed with your request, your attomey advised you that the public generally exception applies
to your facts. According to your attomey, at least 50 percent of the businesses in the
redevelopment area will be affected by the redevelopment decisions in substantially the same
manner as your economic interests. However, the jurisdiction of the city's community
redevelopment commission is the entire city, not just the redevelopment area.e Therefore, to
determine whether the public generally exception applies to a redevelopment decision, you must
consider whether the decision will affect 50 percent of all of the businesses in the City of
Oceanside in substantially the same manner as the business entify that is causing the conflict.
You have not provided any facts regarding the public generally exception.
8. Does the Legally Required Participation Rule apply?
The eighth step is the "legally required participation" rule. (Reg. 18708.) This rule only
applies when several public officials in the same agency have a conflict of interest in the same
decision. The rule does not appear to be relevant to your facts.
8 The legal opinion written by your attomey concludes that a material financial effect is not reasonably
foreseeable. However, the associate memorandum that is attached to the legal opinion states:
"Because the company owns property in the redevelopment area, the likely way it could be affected by a
redevelopment decision is by a resulting increase in the value of its property of more than $10,000 resulting
fromthedecision. The appraisedvalueofSomerset'sproperly...isapproximately$626,000,and$10,000
would represent only a l.60/o incr€as€."
' Under the Act, a local governmental agency's jurisdiction is the geographical area in which the agency
has jurisdiction. (Section 82035.) The jurisdiction of a city redevelopment agency is the city. (Health & Safety
Code $ 33120.)
B. Interrelated and Implementing Decisions
When you are assessing the foreseeable impact of a decision, you must look at each
govemmental decision independently. A finding of a conflict of interest at one stage of a project
does not necessarily mean that you will have a conflict of interest in every decision about that
project. However, a series of decisions may be too interrelated for you to consider separately,
and a conflict on one decision will be disquali$ing for all of the other decisions. Two decisions
are inextricably interrelated when one decision is a necessary condition precedent or condition
subsequent for another.
Furthermore, we have advised that public officials may participate in decisions that
implement major policy decisions already made, despite their financial interests. However, they
may only participate in the implementing decisions if these decisions will not independently
have a material effect on their economic interests. (Curry Advice Letter, No. I-98-006.) For
example, in the Athan Advice Letter, No. A-86-094,we advised:
"[W]e conclude that Mayor Bennett must disqualify himself from
participating in decisions of the San Ramon City
Council/Redevelopment Agency concerning the proposed Crow
Canyon area. However, we emphasize that this conclusion applies
only to the major policy decisions about the project, such as project
boundaries, financing decisions, approval of the environmental
impact report, types of uses, and major public improvements in the
project area. Once the basic policy decisions have been reached,
Mayor Bennett may participate in the decisions which implement,
but do not change these policies."
Similarly, in the Moe Advice Letter, No. A-89-454,we advised that:
"If the decision is one which is critical to the project and could
result in its termination or significant modification, Mr. Abbott
must refrain from participating in it to the extent that the total
effect of the project will materially affect ...[an economic
interestl... according to the test set forth above. If the decision is
one which implements a decision previously made, the effect of
the decision could be measured by assessing the difference
between various implementation alternatives presented."

CONCLUSION: 1. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase Lee &
Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more.
2. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase the fair
market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.
3. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
increase Lee & Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more.
4. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of
the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
increase the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 6, 2000 Colleen C. O'Harra City Councilmember, City of Oceanside Greenman, Lacy, Klein, O'Harra & Heffron 900 Pier View Way Oceanside, California 92049 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-00-174 Dear Councilmember O'Harra: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act.¹ You are seeking reconsideration of the Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-97-567 and the Bennett Advice Letter, No. A-99-307. You are basing your request on new facts, and you have clarified facts upon which these letters relied.² Please note that the analysis in this letter does not evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Reg. 18329(b)(8)(A).) QUESTIONS Your husband works for a real estate agency ("Lee & Associates") that does business in the city's redevelopment area. Your husband also has a client ("Somerset Development Company") that owns property in the redevelopment area. 1. Do you have a conflict of interest in a decision approving the construction of a theater in the redevelopment area (OliverMcMillan project) based on your economic interest in Lee & Associates? 2. Do you have a conflict of interest in the OliverMcMillan decision based on your economic interest in Somerset Development Company? _______________ ¹ See Government Code Sections 81000-91015. Commission regulations appear at California Code of Regulations, Title 2, Sections 18109-18996. ² This letter supersedes both the Hentschke Advice Letter, No. A-97-567 and the Bennett Advice Letter, No. A-99-307 to the extent that they conflict with the advice in this letter. The legal analysis in both letters is still correct. However, you have presented a new set of facts that are significantly different from the facts that were presented on your behalf in the previous letters. This letter is based on the new facts that you have provided. File No. A-00-174 Page No. 2 3. Do you have a conflict of interest in a decision approving a large-scale luxury resort hotel in the redevelopment area (Manchester project) based on your economic interest in Lee & Associates? 4. Do you have a conflict of interest in the Manchester decision based on your economic interest in Somerset Development Company? CONCLUSION 1. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase Lee & Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more. 2. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of the theater unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase the fair market value of Somerset's property by $10,000 or more. 3. You will not have a conflict of interest in the decision to approve the construction of the luxury resort hotel unless it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will increase Lee & Associates' annual gross revenue by $10,000 or more. 4. You will
